sentences.text: Back to Sighet-- arriving back to Sighet was a very traumatic experience because I had to face reality.
There was no other way.
First of all, I should start with one of the most important things, which was shattering.
When I arrived, I-- I was met-- I stayed in the home of a friend of mine who came earlier than I did.
So I stayed in her home and my older brother's brother-in-law, that is, a man by the name of Milikah Stern, heard that I arrived.
He came to see me right away.
And by that time, I somehow learned that my father was taken in the last transport, which was the fourth transport from Sighet, and that he was together with my brother, who was in the small ghetto.
And-- and his wife and his wife's family, they were all together.
And so when Milikah Stern came, it was very clear to me that they were together.
And my first word was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father is alive.
And he said, no.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I kept saying, my father is alive, right?
No, he said, no.
He kept saying no, and I didn't want to hear that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I knew he wasn't alive.
I knew it by then, but I didn't want to know it.
[SIGHS]
And eventually, Milikah Stern confirmed it.
And I said, no, no, he was-- he was healthy.
He was all right.
And he said, look, Bubba, we arrived and we saw the flames.
We arrived-- that particular transport arrived during the day and this-- the father-- they were a very, very religious family.
And when they saw the flames, they all said, [SPEAKING HEBREW], which was-- which is before you die.
Or it's a-- a prayer for the dead.
It's not the Kaddish.
It's [SPEAKING HEBREW].
He said, we saw the flames and your father also said, [SPEAKING HEBREW].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think today, it would be called a nervous breakdown.
I think that's when I let go, and for about two weeks, I just didn't want to go out of the house, nothing, nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And then, I thought that my brothers would come back.
I heard the following story, that my younger brother, Alex, was indeed hiding in Budapest but that he was-- one day, he went out, and he was recognized by one of his schoolmates by the name of [?
Strimbay, ?] who sported a huge swastika and who was part of the Arrow-- Arrowhead, which was the Hungarian Nazi party, and recognized my brother and said, Fischman, what are you doing here?
Come.
And my brother said to him, look, we sat next to each other in class.
Turn your head, and I'll disappear.
No, he took him.

And I was told that in February of 1945, when my brother was caught, at that time, it was known by then that the Hungarians just massacred the Jews and threw them in-- into the Danube and that-- and corpses were floating, floating for days, and that this is where my brother Alex found his end.
Then, I began inquiring about my older brother, what happened to him.
That I was told and that was confirmed, he worked at the [?
Buna ?] factory.
And when the Russians were approaching Auschwitz, just around the middle of January, they were liquidating the camp and trying to do away with-- with the evidence of genocide.
And my brother was put in a column death march with a certain Dr.
Kraus, who was a doctor in our town, and the conditions were terrible.
And he must have been emotionally completely at his wit's end, and they were marching in the snow.
And he gave up-- he must have known by then that his little son was not alive, and he must have thought that his wife, whose name was-- is-- Lajchu is not alive, either.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He knew by then than his parents were not alive.
He-- I don't know what he thought about me, but I'm-- I'm-- this-- this is just conjecture.
So he gave up hope, and he refused to walk and the SS shot him in the snow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is true.
A man by the name of Dr.
Verner, who was in the same column who did survive.
And Dr.
Kraus was also shot later on.
He was an older man.
He could not go.
But my brother just gave up hope.

The irony of it is that while his little boy did not survive-- and I know this directly from his wife, my former sister-in-law, Lajchu, when they were pulled out of the trains and separated, whether it was one of the inmates of Auschwitz, a man or whoever it was came to her and said, don't hold on to your child.
And she was a young woman.
She was 25, probably, and she was with her mother, whom I remember as being an elderly woman, a small, fat lady.
And she gave the little boy to her mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Lajchu survived.
She came home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but my brother did not.
Then, as I said, I had about two weeks of-- of what would be considered-- because I just didn't want to get out of bed, and I just lay there.
And I didn't want to see anybody.
And I couldn't cry, and that was the worst.
I-- I was just laying there and I really believed that Stern, Milikah is making up these stories.
No, my-- he'll-- he'll come home someday, somehow.
Eventually, after two weeks, some of my friends came and said, look, Barbara, there is the Joint, you know.
We are-- let's see what we can do.
They pulled me out, and I went to the Joint and the Joint was very-- very-- very supportive, very supportive.
And then, I went back to see my parents' house, and that was a horrible experience.
All the windows and the doors were ripped out.
The parquet floor was ripped up.
People were-- have-- not only did they loot and empty the house of furniture and everything, but they also pulled up, as I said, the parquet floors to see if we didn't hide something.
Because they knew that Fischman was a fairly wealthy man and things-- you know, the Jew hid things.
So I couldn't live there.
By this time, my sister-- sister-in-law and of course, my other sister-in-law was very close to me.
And I knew both my brothers did not survive, so it seemed to me at that time.

And my then sister-in-law, this Lajchu, her family's house was fairly intact because this Milikah Stern who was liberated in Buchenwald in April, he somehow managed to come home very, very soon thereafter, and he found his house, his parents' house.
He even, I was told, did something which he shouldn't have done.
He opened my father's store and said that this now belongs to him.
And he did something he shouldn't have done.
But my sister-in-law came back, and she asked me because I was living with another girlfriend of mine.
She said that, no, no, this doesn't look good.
You come and stay with me, with us, which I did.
And some of her sisters came back, so there was some sort of a community.
But I-- I was really very much out of it for the whole month of this return.

Particularly, I was-- not only I, all the-- as the survivors began coming back, sort of dribbling in, we recognized a woman, a Hungarian woman walking with my dress, with my sister-in-law's shoes, with this, that.
I saw a woman there riding my bike, and it was terrible because, you know, it's mine.
Well, why did you come back?
It was given to us, and it was resentment on the part of the population for the Jews having come back and saying, hey, this is my armoire, as I found my-- my armoire in my own room with the Dandier.
All right?
And so there was this kind of a terrible thing, and also the Russians were in charge because it was-- the Russians were the liberators.
And I remember Stalin's huge pictures, looking very grandfatherly with the pipe in his mouth, you know, bedecking all the big buildings.
And it was-- it was very sad.
It was very sad, you know?
That morning when I was liberated, that beautiful May morning, I thought that when I'll come back home, people will throw flowers.
Thank God that you survived, but this was not the case.
They didn't want us back.
And then, one day-- it was July, I believe.
I sort of-- you know, I don't know how I lived frankly.
The Joint subsidized us, you know, gave us money and-- for the war.
By this time, you see, it was Romania again, because the Hungarians were part of the Axis and the Romanians, they're very smart in switching in the last moment.
So now, they were the great Russian allies.
And so there was the Kommandatura.
It was very much Russian-run, and the Romanians were nominally in charge.
But the Communist party really took over, the Romanian Communist Party.
And one day, I went to-- it was some sort of a little drugstore that a friend of mine, a young man, opened, and we used to gather there.
It was sort of a [NON-ENGLISH] meaning a gathering, a watering hole, sort of, where the survivors would come and schmooze and chat.
And-- but before then, it-- we began doing something.
At night, we would go to somebody's home, gather together, and drink and get drunk.
And we would sing songs that the Nazis would sing against us, the Hungarian Nazis.
I remember one particular song.
We would sing the melody, and the original song was saying something to the effect, [HUNGARIAN], 'one Jew, two Jews, we just hung the chief Jew,' something of that sort.
Well, we would take the melody and twist it around and sing, 'one Nazi, two Nazi, we just hung the chief Nazi.' But we would get drunk night after night, night after night.
And somehow, this is how, you know, we spent our nights and in many-- many nights, we were in somebody else's house.
No, we were screaming it on the top of our lungs, on the top of our lungs to get it out, to get it out.
We didn't know how to cry, I think, how to mourn.
And this was one way, I think.
There was so much pain, so much bitterness, and so much anger and encountering this?
And that-- that's why I said that it was the height of my idealism and the end of it, because I came home to this.

And then, one nice day-- May-- I went-- the washerwoman that we had who was awfully decent, she brought to me one morning some-- my mother's fur coat and some items that apparently, my mother gave her to keep.
And she was very decent, and she kept it.
And she brought it back to me.
But she also brought a big basket of cherries from her own orchard or whatever.
So I went with that basket of cherries to this friend's little drugstore [NON-ENGLISH] gathering hole because I knew that I'd find some-- some survivors there.
I mean, we had nobody.
We had no parents.
We were-- we were young kids, not yet 20, and-- and-- and what do you do?
And I walked in and I had this basket in my hands.
And I said, come on, guys, girls, here.
And they each took cherries.
And then, I heard the voice in Romanian-- because we were talking Hungarian-- in Romanian, well, miss, don't I get any cherries?
And I look and the voice comes from the back of the store and comes forth a very, very handsome Russian officer, very handsome.
But more than his handsomeness, what I remembered was the smiling eyes.
And I said, sure, and he had some cherries.

Well, clearly, a romance , began but it began in-- in a very painful way because that afternoon, he invited the entire-- the entire group to go to some park near the river, which was a very famous park that we all went.
And he kept telling us, talk, talk.
We all spoke Romanian, and we-- we talked.
And it turned out that he himself was a Romanian Jew born in Iasi, 'Ias-i' as it's pronounced here, which was part of Romania where there was a terrible pogrom, I believe, in 1942.
I think so.
But-- but he was in his youth.
He was a young man.
He was about 26, I believe.
While he was a student in Iasi, he became a Communist, very simple.

And when in 1940, Bessarabia was ceded to Russia-- this was also that reward, that Vienna award where all kinds of things were going on, chopping up countries back and forth-- he went over to Russia because he was a Communist.
He was an idealist, a Communist idealist.
And so now, when this war starts, he is in the Russian army.
And then, we learned that he found out about his parents, about the pogrom, but nobody knew what happened to his family.
His name was-- I hope it still is-- Leonid Geller, Geller.

He was looking for the Geller family, and nobody could tell him what happened to them, whether they perished in the pogrom, in the Iasi pogrom, or whether they were taken away because they were also-- the Romanians took people to Transnistria where they set them up in ghettos and so forth.
And as he said, as he was rolling down with the Russian army, he scoured every camp on his way to look for his parents, and he never found them.
And that evening, he invited me to a dance, and I went and a romance started.
And I was very fearful [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I don't want to lose again.
I have had so many losses but there was something else.
He helped me cry for the first time.
And he was telling me how he went from camp to camp looking for his parents and how he knew what I went through.
And he looked at my number, and that's when I cried.
And he said, you know, [?
Bubika, ?] if I could lessen your pain by taking that number onto me, I would.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the first time in all that time that somebody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shared with me and-- and-- and wanted to make me feel better and understood and understood.
But there was a problem, because Sighet being a fairly small town, it was not very well-regarded that a Russian-- I mean, you know, he didn't have a sign saying, I'm Jewish.
But, I mean, he wore the red uniform.
So first of all, it was a terrible thing that this Fischman [? boy ?] is involved with a Russian officer.
That was not too good.
And from his point of view, particularly since where my sister-in-law lived and I lived with them, the Russian Kommandatura was just across the street.
And Leonid would come regularly to visit, and-- and-- you know, he would take me.
I saw Popov, the-- the big Russian clown, because he came to entertain the army.
So he too was in a bit of hot water, but it didn't matter.
It didn't matter.
For two months, my pain was alleviated because the only thing that can alleviate-- not forget-- is love.
The only thing that can alleviate pain, mute pain, is love.
And two months thereafter, however, his-- his captain or-- or superior, his [RUSSIAN], said, I cannot-- I cannot cover up for you anymore.
And this, his superior, a-- a [RUSSIAN] by the name of [?
Stern ?] was a Communist and a Jew.
And I remember he showed me the-- the-- red-- that's the only authentic Communist card I saw.
[RUSSIAN] [?
Stern ?] showed it to me.
And he would come even so, with all his Communist-- he was part of the 1918 whatever.
He-- he was one of the Communists, of the old-time Communists.
He would still come to my sister-in-law's house and bring, I don't know, chocolates or something and say to us, talk to me in Yiddish.
You know, this was this great Communist who said, talk to me in Yiddish because there was nobody there.
But after a while, clearly, there were higher-ups than he.

He told Leonid, I cannot cover up for you anymore, and-- and you have to stop seeing her, which he refused, whereupon he was sent in to a-- to the Donbass, which was sort of a punitive colony in the Ukraine, where they placed, you know, the not very reliable elements.
It was not the Gulag, by no means, because he was still in the Russian army, Leonid, but it was very rough.
And it was a very sad parting, a very sad parting, but we parted.
And then, like within two weeks, one morning, we look out the window, and we see someone on horseback, and it's Leonid.
Somehow, he managed to come back, and he managed to get permission to marry this girl.
But he said something.
He said that I want you to know that life under the Communist regime is not what you know.
And he described to me the life.
As we now know, it was drab, uniform, no-- whatever, which didn't bother me.
But what I did was consult with my sister-in-law.
What do I do?
And this is what she said to me.
At that time, we did not yet have my older brother's knowledge of what happened to him because we were still waiting for him.
She said to me like this, she said, Barbara, when your brother, [?
Manot, ?] my husband, comes home, and he finds out that he lost his son, he lost his mother and father, and his-- and [?
Shonie, ?] Alex, we've heard that he was in-- on the Danube.
He was thrown into the Danube and his only living member of his family, his sister, his little sister is in Russia, how can I tell him that?
How can you do that?
And I did not go.
The sad part of it is that-- that soon thereafter, we found out about the end.
This is when Dr.
Verner came home and told us what happened.
But very soon thereafter, I-- I-- I was devastated, devastated that-- that I lost-- now again, I lost everything.
I lost this man with whom I was in love and who was so good to me and who has showed me how to cry, and I don't have a brother.
And my other brother is surely not alive, the Danube. 1945, December-- or maybe it was 1946, January-- it doesn't really matter-- at night, somebody knocks on the window.
We run, and I look out, and it's my brother Alex.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] So there was some sort of an evilness.
What had happened, he was not shot.
But he was put in a transport, and he himself is going to give a testimony soon.
He was eventually sent to a work camp in-- called [?
Einegamme, ?] [?
Einegamme, ?] and he was liberated by the British.
Now, he began looking at all kinds of Red Cross lists for names, and he found no names.
Clearly, he-- the Russians didn't keep lists.
So he had no particular compunction to come back home until somebody-- and this was at the end of December 1945 or early January 1946-- met him.
This somebody left-- had left Sighet and met my brother somewhere in western Europe and said, hey, Alex, do you know your sister is back home in Sighet?
That's when he came back, and that's how we met.
And just as far as my brother is concerned, he stayed for a couple of months.
He stayed for-- for-- to the end of the year.
Then, he went back because then already, the-- the Israelis that-- he eventually volunteered the-- into the Haganah during the War of Independence.
As far as I'm concerned, this-- it was a very drab life because the Communist Party was after me to join and-- the Romanian Communist Party.
I was young, and they needed cadres.
And I didn't really want to because I didn't know much about Communism, but I didn't want to-- or any -isms.
But I didn't want to join, whether it's Fascism, Communism.
This to me, I don't want to join.
And I kept postponing and postponing, and I'll think and I'll do this.
Well, I am not so sure.
The pressure became eventually terrible.
And after my brother-- while my brother was still dead, somehow, we managed.
But after my brother left, I said to myself, now, I don't want this.
You know?
I just want to leave.

And eventually-- again, my uncle, the one who was waiting for refugees at the Joint in Budapest, he arranged that I join a group who would smuggle through many, many borders, Romania, Hungary, Hungary, Austria, Austria, US-occupied Germany, to Munich, where one of his daughters was.
And she was the one who told me about my brother Alex, because when he left, he too went the same route and stayed with her and her husband.
At that time, they just got married, because that was her childhood sweetheart.
And she was the one who told me that my brother joined the Haganah and to Marseilles and from there, he was fighting the War of Independence.
